title: Lost and Found
desc: At Castle Bodhran, mislaid property and mislaid travelers tend to collect in the same office.

steps.text:
The lost-and-found room at Castle Bodhran contains the usual things - gloves, satchels, maps, travel pins - and the unusual ones, like a single boot labeled "recovered from the chandelier route" and a room key tied to a note that reads FOUND OWNERLESS, OWNER LIKELY ALSO OWNERLESS. The clerk managing the shelf takes all of this with practiced calm.

"Visitors drop things," she says, dipping a pen into ink. "Then they go looking for them. Then they forget where they began looking. The castle helps, in its own way." On her desk sit two current problems: a satchel tagged with a faded room token and a boot reported missing by a merchant who is currently certain the castle has stolen it personally.

The clerk slides both notes toward you. "Trace the satchel by its room token, or retrieve the missing boot from the balcony turn where it was last seen. Either would improve the overall mood of this building."

options.label: Trace the satchel to its proper room and owner
Recover the missing boot from the balcony turn

steps.text:
The room token is faded but readable: blue stripe, three brass dots, guest wing east. You follow the current route markings through a corridor that becomes a stairwell and then reappears as a hall lined with doors set at politely incorrect angles. Room eighteen belongs to a scholar who opens the door already apologizing.

"Please tell me you found a satchel with two notebooks and a very fragile opinion of my organizational skills."

You present the satchel. The scholar's relief is immediate and disproportionate, which means the notebooks mattered. "I thought I'd left it by the breakfast hall," they admit. "Then I realized the breakfast hall I remembered may have been a ceiling passage."

Back in the lost-and-found room, the clerk records the return with a crisp line of ink. "Good," she says. "Objects should not remain lost longer than their owners. It encourages bad habits in both."

options.label: Return the receipt and clear one more shelf space

steps.text:
The balcony turn is a narrow service route overlooking the entry hall, notable for its excellent acoustics and poor relationship with loose footwear. The missing boot has landed on a ledge just below the handrail where an ordinary ladder cannot reach but a retrieval hook can.

You brace yourself against the rail, lower the hook, and coax the boot toward you inch by inch. It catches twice on decorative stonework clearly designed by someone who had never once considered laundry or luggage. On the third try it lifts free, swings once, and lands squarely in your hand.

The merchant waiting below receives it with the dignity of someone pretending not to have spent ten minutes accusing architecture of malice. "Thank you," he says. After a pause, he adds, "I still maintain the castle was involved."

"The castle is always involved," the clerk says when you return. She enters the recovery in her ledger and sets the boot's claim chit aside. "That is not the same as guilt."

options.label: Hang up the retrieval hook and close the case
